Diogo Jota honoured in Portugal game. England, meanwhile, had plenty to celebrate - a double from Harry Kane, with Jude Bellingham and Marcus Rashford also on the scoresheet in a 4-2 win over Croatia. In one of the late dramas of the day, Caleb Yirenkyi fires home a stunning stoppage-time winner to give Ghana a 1-0 victory over Panama. Join your host Haylena Krishnamoorthy for this episode of World Cup Daily | The 90+ Podcast, as she gives you the biggest talking points from day seven of the World Cup. items.title: Digitisation and AI being used to chart the world's plant and fungi population
items.summary: More than 400 scientists in 40 countries have used digital tools to help identify plants and fungi species, analyse historical specimens and detect changes linked to climate change. items.title: How secure is the peace deal between the US and Iran?
items.summary: The United Nations has welcomed the peace deal between the U-S and Iran, acknowledging that the agreement represents a critical step towards the peaceful settlement of the conflict. But Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has distanced his government from the peace deal, saying he respects the decision but will continue to protect his own country's interests by taking action against Hezbollah in Lebanon. The peace deal depends on a ceasefire on all fronts of the war - which includes Lebanon. So how secure is the deal?

Lifeblood is urging more Australians to donate blood, warning the need is constant and cannot be met without regular donors. But as Australia's population becomes more diverse, experts say the country's blood supply also needs to reflect that change, with some patients needing blood that is more closely matched to their background.

items.title: Fallout from UK minister's resignation casts doubt on AUKUS deal

The political fallout is now being felt on both sides of the world, with critics questioning the stability of one of Australia's most ambitious defence projects.

items.summary: A 'worrying' trend is emerging in Australia's flu response, experts warn.

items.title: AI IPO rush meets market jitters as Austrailans rethink where to put their savings
items.summary: The global AI investment boom is gathering pace, with OpenAI signalling its intention to eventually join rivals Anthropic and SpaceX on the US sharemarket, potentially fuelling another wave of capital raising worth hundreds of billions of dollars across the industry. SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Loftus Peak Chief Investment Officer Alex Pollak about what the AI listing race means for investors, while Adam Dawes from Shaw and Partners breaks down a softer day on the ASX after the long weekend. Plus, Matthew Hassan, Senior Economist at Westpac, explains why Australians are turning away from property as the wisest place for savings and what weakening consumer sentiment could mean for the economy.

items.title: Survivors recount experiences after 7.8 earthquake | SBS News in Easy English 9 June 2026

items.summary: Pharmacists want to reduce need to book a doctor's appointment for a quick script, but GP's aren't convinced.

items.title: The $400k super line that could define how comfortable retirement is
items.summary: If you want a comfortable retirement, it's vital to look at more than one factor from your fund, a new report suggests.

items.title: Here's what to watch out for this tax time — and when it’s better to lodge
items.summary: This year, the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) has warned against relying on “tax hacks” from “finfluencers”.

items.title: State funeral for renowned melanoma pathologist Richard Scolyer
items.summary: Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has paid tribute to world-renowned melanoma pathologist Richard Scolyer, describing him as a "truly remarkable man" and announcing a state funeral will be held. The 2024 Australian of the Year died on Sunday aged 59, three years after being diagnosed with brain cancer.

items.title: Violence in Middle East following Israeli strikes on Lebanon
items.summary: The Israeli Defence Force says it has struck military targets in western and central Iran, after Iran launched a series of strikes on northern Israel. The latest exchange follows Israeli strikes in Beirut’s southern suburbs, which Lebanese health officials say killed at least two and injured at least 20.

items.summary: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

items.title: Philippines earthquake kills at least 35, dozens missing as thousands wait to return home
items.summary: A powerful earthquake shook part of the southern Philippines on Monday, causing at least 35 deaths.

items.title: Philippines earthquake kills at least 35, dozens missing as thousands wait to return home
items.summary: A powerful earthquake shook part of the southern Philippines on Monday, causing at least 35 deaths.

items.title: Israel and Iran trade strikes in first clash since truce despite Trump's call for restraint
items.summary: Israeli airstrikes have been reported in at least three Iranian cities after Iran launched a barrage of missiles at Israel.

items.title: Lindy Chamberlain says jurors 'didn't understand' the evidence in her case. She wants change
items.summary: Twelve members of the community can decide the fate of those on trial. Is the jury system still effective?

items.title: Hundreds of community heroes celebrated in the 2026 King's Birthday Honours
items.summary: More than 900 Australians have been recognised for their exceptional contribution to the country in the 2026 King's Birthday Honours List. Here, SBS spotlights some of the many community heroes celebrated with a Medal of the Order of Australia. Among them are a self-defence teacher, a powerful advocate for victims of crime, and an author celebrating historic achievements of Greek women.

items.title: King's Birthday Honours 2026: See the full list of awardees
items.summary: From sport and the arts to academia and industry, the King's Birthday Honours celebrate Australia's best. See the full list.

items.title: Players sent off, matches abandoned: FIFA to crack down on discrimination at the World Cup
items.summary: The FIFA World Cup 2026 could be a test for the organisation and its commitment to rid football of racism. In 2024, FIFA launched its 'Global Stand Against Racism' with a series of initiatives, including an anti-racism panel, education campaigns, and introducing tougher rules to combat discrimination. With incidents of racism continuing to blight professional football while FIFA prepares for its biggest tournament ever in Mexico, Canada and the US, here's how the footballing organisation's policies may play out.

items.title: This island's population is the same as Darwin's. It's about to play in the World Cup
items.summary: Curaçao has the same population as Darwin — and it's competing in the World Cup. What could the 'Blue Wave' mean for its future?

items.title: Public health agency concerned latest Ebola outbreak could become largest on record
items.summary: The World Health Organisation says the outbreak is 'moving fast'.

items.title: Australia's fuel supply secured well into August as bowser pressure eases
items.summary: But with the government's fuel tax set to increase again, the relief could be temporary.

items.title: Whose choice is it? The unseen side of Australia's abortion debate
items.summary: Some pregnant people face harassment and bullying from family members pushing for termination.

items.title: Putin says 'no point' meeting with Zelenskyy, vowing to double down on war efforts
items.summary: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy had called for a sit-down between the two leaders to end the four-year war.

items.title: Interview: Dr Sam Bennett from Grattan Institute on future of the NDIS
items.summary: Australians who use the National Disability Insurance Scheme are facing an anxious wait with political gridlock in Canberra. The Federal Government, Greens and Coalition have been considering post-budget measures on tax and the NDIS and there's just one sitting fortnight remaining before Parliament rises for a five-week Winter break. As negotiations continue, Tee Mitchell spoke with Dr Sam Bennett, the Disability Program Director at the Grattan Institute. Dr Bennett starts by addressing the elephant in the room which is what might happen if the proposed changes don't make it through the parliament.

items.title: Torres Strait’s first land and sea conference weaves culture with science
items.summary: Traditional owners in Far North Queensland have come together with scientists for the first time to help tackle threats against human-induced climate change. The inaugural conference with leaders from seventeen different Torres Strait Islands is helping shape Australia's next State of Environment Report due in December this year. Reporter Josh van Staden travelled to Thursday Island with support from the Pulitzer Center.

items.title: The $1 million question facing Australians planning for retirement
items.summary: New retirement findings highlight growing financial concerns. Plus: Incoming pay rises, and Australia's property market shift.

items.title: Sell before 2027 or hold forever? Australians weigh impact of CGT changes
items.summary: Tax changes unveiled in the budget have rattled many Australians. Here's what to consider if you want to sell an asset.

items.title: As home sellers slash prices, one of Sydney's richest suburbs records the biggest discount
items.summary: Despite price falls in certain suburbs, an economist says one factor of the property market remains unchanged.

items.title: Tumbling miners lead ASX to a down week
items.summary: The ASX 200 closed 0.7% lower on Friday, extending its weekly decline to 1.2%, as mining stocks came under pressure from a three-month low in iron ore prices. Fortescue, BHP and Rio Tinto all fell, while the major banks also weakened. Bucking the trend was healthcare giant CSL, which jumped 5.8% for its strongest gain in more than four years. SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Blueberry Markets analyst Zoran Kresovic about the forces driving the market and what investors are watching next.

items.title: SBS News in Easy English 05 June 2026
Hanson steps in after Joyce backflips on forced home sales
items.summary: After failing to answer questions, a senator was warned to "get some clarity before this gets worse".

items.title: Hezbollah rejects renewed ceasefire in Lebanon as Trump maintains the deal holds
items.summary: Hezbollah has rejected a US-backed renewed ceasefire deal between Israel and Lebanon. The leader of the Iranian-backed group says the terms were negotiated in talks that they were excluded from - and they amount to surrender.

items.title: Trump announces $981 million support package for highly polluting energy source
items.summary: The White House has hosted politicians and governors from coal-rich states to announce further support for "beautiful, clean coal."

items.title: Zelenskyy seizes opportunity as Russia concedes weaknesses in air defence
items.summary: Zelenskyy's proposal comes after heavy Ukranian drone attacks on St. Petersburg prompted the Kremlin's surprising admission.

items.title: Israeli strikes kill nine in Gaza, despite ceasefire
items.summary: At least 15 others were wounded in overnight attacks that targeted four apartments.

items.title: SpaceX’s mega IPO lands as miners drag the market lower
items.summary: Elon Musk has revealed plans for a potentially record-breaking IPO of SpaceX, with Australian retail investors set to get access to the offer. Stephanie Youssef speaks with Datt Capital Chief Investment Officer Emanuel Datt about the opportunity and the risks for investors. Plus, the Australian sharemarket falls 1.1 per cent as mining stocks lead the losses, with Robert Talevski from Activam Group breaking down the day’s market moves and what drove the sell-off.

items.title: Queensland children under five to be removed from residential state care
items.summary: A Queensland Commission of Inquiry has delivered a damning report into the state's child protection system. It detailed evidence of sexual abuse in residential care settings, and delivered 52 recommendations.The government has announced it will remove all children under five from residential care, and respond to the recommendations within two months.

items.title: Trump’s Bibi tirade, Hegseth peddles used subs &… lucky testicles?
items.summary: Donald Trump loses his patience with Benjamin Netanyahu over Lebanon, while the Israeli prime minister hints at an even bigger endgame in Gaza. Is a new Middle East deal within reach, or the region’s latest escalation heading to another full conflict? Plus, a surprise AUKUS announcement on the sidelines of the Shangri-La dialogue and how an iconic Italian artwork is hanging in there.

items.title: Can you negatively gear a granny flat? Labor has given an answer
items.summary: Questions raised during Senate estimates about negative gearing and granny flats have finally been answered.

items.title: 'Horrific': Penny Wong grilled on investigation into alleged sexual assault of flotilla activist
items.summary: Fronting a Senate estimates hearing, the foreign minister was also asked how many people Australia has helped to leave Gaza.

items.title: 'Pacific ashtray': Fiji rejects Australian billionaire's plan to ship and burn waste
items.summary: A proposal to ship non-recyclable rubbish to Fiji from across the region for burning has been labelled "waste colonialism".

items.title: SBS News in Easy English 04 June 2026
Racial furore erupts after video emerges of UK police handcuffing dying stab victim
items.summary: A WARNING THAT THIS STORY MAY BE DISTRESSING FOR SOME LISTENERS Britain's interior minister has accused activists of hijacking a tragedy to stir up violence after the death of a teenager who was handcuffed as he lay dying - while his killer falsely claimed a racist attack. Nowak's death has triggered debates about policing and knife crime and has spurred claims by far-right activists and politicians that there is bias against white people in the justice system.

items.title: 'Not the act of a friendly country': Bipartisan anger erupts over 'rotten' US tariff plan
items.summary: The United States has proposed a new set of tariffs on 60 trading partners, including Australia, alleging they have failed to impose and enforce forced labour bans in their supply chains. The Australian government has rejected the accusations and says the proposed 12.5 per cent import tax contravenes the free trade agreement.

items.title: Medicinal cannabis patients given green light to drive in NSW
items.summary: The state government is opening up legal access to the roads for patients who have taken cannabis medication.

items.title: The World Cup is finding out what fans are worth in football's new economy
items.summary: Some fans have been priced out of the World Cup in North America, which some critics say is part of a wider shift in sporting culture.

items.title: Australia's economy stalls despite data centre investment boom
items.summary: Australia’s economy expanded by just 0.3% in the March quarter, with growth driven largely by investment in data centres while household spending remained subdued and productivity declined. NAB Senior Markets Economist Taylor Nugent joins SBS On the Money to unpack what the latest GDP figures mean for the economy and the outlook for interest rates. Plus, despite the weak economic data, the ASX 200 climbed 0.7% as investors scaled back expectations of a near-term rate rise. Josh Gilbert from eToro explains what drove the market higher, including record highs for mining giants BHP and Rio Tinto, strong gains for the banks and a pullback in technology stocks.

The blue-tongued lizard at the heart of one school's reconciliation journey
items.summary: Behind one school's blue-tongued lizard lies a deeper story about kinship and Country.

items.title: CSIRO confirms significant job cuts due to rising science costs
items.summary: Executive staff said they can meet climate change research obligations despite restricting their workforce.

items.title: Minimum wage workers get a pay rise as Anthropic looks to list on the sharemarket
items.summary: Around three million low-paid Australian workers are set to receive a pay rise after the Fair Work Commission lifted the national minimum wage and increased award wages above the current inflation rate. Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Commonwealth Bank economist Harry Ottley about what the decision means for household budgets, inflation and the broader economy. Plus, the ASX 200 edged lower as retailers and shopping centre operators came under pressure following the wage decision, while technology stocks rallied and BHP hit another record high. Alice Shen, CFA from VanEck joins the podcast to unpack the day’s market moves and the influence of another strong session on Wall Street

items.title: Wage umpire's 'regret' over minimum pay rise as unions cheer, employers cry foul
items.summary: Unions had been calling for a 6 per cent increase, which employers branded a "ludicrous" ask.

items.title: Climate change 'loading the dice' as farmers brace for El Niño announcement
items.summary: A formal El Niño declaration could pivot many Australian regions back into prolonged dry spells.

items.title: The Australian fans paying up to $25,000 for their FIFA World Cup 2026 'dream'
items.summary: Soaring flight, accommodation and ticket prices have not deterred thousands of Australians heading to this year's FIFA World Cup.

items.title: Melbourne's love of head-to-toe black clothing actually makes sense. Here's why
items.summary: Melbourne has built a reputation for its love of black outfits. It's not a random phenomenon.

items.title: Israel orders troops further into Lebanon following day of heavy fire by Hezbollah
items.summary: Israeli troops have pushed further into Lebanon despite the ceasefire announced six weeks ago.

items.title: Labor's problem, One Nation's gain: What's driving the poll surge
items.summary: One Nation is now the nation's most popular party a new poll suggests, which could offer lessons for the government.

items.title: What to know about Beaufort Castle — the 900-year-old site seized by Israel in Lebanon
items.summary: The fortress has been a strategic military asset for centuries.

items.title: Ceasefire hopes fade as tensions persist
items.summary: Despite weeks of diplomacy and repeated claims a broader agreement is close, conflict continues across the Middle East. Israeli forces have expanded operations in Lebanon, civilians have again been killed in Gaza, and uncertainty remains over negotiations between Iran and the United States.

items.title: 'Very excited': New discovery in Laos cave rescue as Aussie diver outlines next steps
items.summary: South Australian diver Josh Richards says miners freed from a flooded cave in Laos had provided valuable information to rescuers.

items.title: When Lisa was diagnosed with dementia in her 40s, her partner Luke had to 'beg' for support
items.summary: Younger-onset dementia is rare but can occur when some are still looking after children and accessing government support can be difficult.

items.title: Devastating storm set to move across Australia after leaving thousands in WA without power
items.summary: The powerful weather event is being described as a "once in five-year" storm and is bringing winds of up to 125kph.

items.title: SBS Speaks to Dr Marianne Jauncey about NSW's only medically supervised injecting centre
items.summary: New South Wales’ only medically supervised injecting centre is calling for urgent legal reform to allow more sites to open across the state. It has been 25 years since the centre opened in Sydney’s Kings Cross, becoming the first supervised injecting facility in the Southern Hemisphere. Staff have since supervised more than 1.3 million injections, managed more than 12-thousand overdoses without a single death, and made more than 25-thousand referrals for treatment and care. For more, SBS spoke with the centre's Medical Director Dr Marianne Jauncey.

items.title: Report calls for more federal support for new dads
items.summary: A report calling for more federal support for new dads was tabled in federal parliament this week by the Special Envoy for Men's Health Dan Repacholi. The report by the Movember Institute surveyed 1,200 Australian fathers, finding 84 per cent said fatherhood gave their life greater meaning and 77 per cent said it helped them express love and vulnerability more. But three of five new fathers said they were never asked about their mental health, while one in five reported feeling more isolated or lonely since becoming a father.

items.title: The ancient Japanese skills helping young people combat modern stress
items.summary: Almost half of all teenagers in Australia are facing psychological distress, according to recent studies. Japanese martial arts skills are helping some build resilience.

items.title: Dating apps are turning to AI. These Australians have a better idea
items.summary: Dating apps have been losing users in recent years. What's emerging, experts say, isn't just a trend.

items.title: Autism may be as common in women as men. Yet many go undiagnosed
items.summary: Autism has long been seen as a condition affecting boys. Researchers say that understanding is finally changing.

items.title: Australian flotilla participants join ICC case, alleging abuse in Israeli custody
items.summary: Eleven Australians were detained by Israel while on board a flotilla attempting to deliver aid to Gaza.

items.title: House prices are falling. So why are many Australians still struggling to buy?
items.summary: Borrowing power is shrinking faster than house prices, leaving many buyers no closer to owning a home.

items.title: 'A battle of resilience': Why these teachers and youngsters are looking at karate differently
items.summary: In a Sydney dojo, karate is being used to build more than physical strength.

items.title: Has climbing Mount Everest become too commercialised and dangerous?
items.summary: Nepal's mountaineering community is celebrating the 73rd anniversary of the conquest of Mount Everest amid growing concerns about overcrowding, rising temperatures. Nepal has issued a record 494 permits for foreign climbers, with the number of people who reached the summit believed to be more than 900. Questions are growing over whether the world's highest mountain is becoming too dangerous, and too commercialised.

items.title: Benjamin Netanyahu says Israel will expand control of Gaza to 70 per cent
items.summary: The Israeli prime minister said increasing control of Palestinian territory will "press" Hamas from "all sides".

items.title: 'For us, by us': Indigenous plea for truth-telling and connection during Reconciliation Week
items.summary: Community led events are being held across the country to mark National Reconciliation Week, which runs from May 27 to Jun 03. This year's theme is 'All In' - a call for all Australians to commit wholeheartedly to reconciliation, and to advancing Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people's rights.

items.title: Is 'baby brain' real? Nearly 300 parents put the theory to the test
items.summary: Many new parents describe the mental fog of early parenthood as 'baby brain'. But Australian researchers found a surprise.

items.title: Fire in Kenya girls' school dormitory kills 16, injures 73
items.summary: Local authorities said the fire broke out in a dormitory where 220 girls had been sleeping.

items.title: Iran-US peace talks stall, Türkiye targets dissent & Trump’s excellent excuse
items.summary: Conflicting reports on peace negotiations between the US and Iran as military tensions escalate across the region. Former political prisoner Dr Kylie Moore-Gilbert joins us to explore what it means for the Iranian regime and its citizens. Plus, Netanyahu vows to crush Hezbollah with an expanding offensive as his own leadership comes under pressure ahead of elections. While in Türkiye, Erdogan moves against the opposition in a fresh crackdown on dissent, and Trump’s patriotic excuse for dodging a family function.

items.title: Detainees at US immigration centre on hunger strike amid concern over conditions, high suicide rates
items.summary: Hundreds of people in an immigration detention centre in the US state of New Jersey are on a hunger strike, according to their families. They're protesting poor conditions inside the Delaney Hall facility. It comes as an investigation found 10 people have died by suicide in immigration detention since President Donald Trump took office at the beginning of last year and launched an aggressive deportation strategy.

items.title: Federal government sues 3M over PFAS contamination
items.summary: The Australian government is suing multinational industrial company 3M in the largest ever claim brought by the Commonwealth. The legal action will seek damages in relation to PFAS contamination across defence sites throughout Australia, alleging the company withheld information about their products.

items.title: Will Australia follow the US and Canada on Ebola travel restrictions?
items.summary: The Department of Health says it is "closely monitoring" the situation.

items.title: Seven dead, officials on alert as Europe swelters through spring that feels like summer
items.summary: Authorities in France say at least seven people have died due to unseasonably warm temperatures, while for the second day in a row, the UK has recorded a new record high for May. Much of Western Europe is still dealing with a pre-summer heatwave, leading to health warnings across the continent.

items.title: Reform or rebrand? Advocates say employment system is still unfit for work
items.summary: The government has announced what it has described as the biggest reform to employment services in over 30 years. The system has been set up to help unemployed Australians find work. But with the continuation of mutual obligations and private providers, advocates say the changes are nothing more than a rebrand.

items.title: Newly elected Solomon Islands prime minister set to visit Australia in first official trip
items.summary: Matthew Wale was elected prime minister in May following a no-confidence vote against his predecessor.

items.title: 'Shameful': Outrage as New Zealand scraps its standalone environment department
items.summary: Legal changes will now see the environment managed under a "mega-ministry" covering housing, urban development, and transport.

items.title: Hamas confirms Israeli strikes killed its new military leader in Gaza
items.summary: Thousands of people gathered in Gaza City for the joint funeral of Mohammed Odeh and members of his family.
